name: Umiam Lake
description: Spread across 220 sq km, Umiam Lake is a stunning reservoir created by damming the Umiam river. Surrounded by pine-covered hills, it offers boating, kayaking, and breathtaking sunsets.
tip: Arrive early morning for misty reflections and fewer crowds. Water sports are available on weekends.

name: Elephant Falls
description: A three-tiered waterfall surrounded by dense vegetation, named by the British for a rock shaped like an elephant (which was destroyed by an earthquake). A must-visit natural wonder.
tip: Wear non-slip footwear — the paths can be slippery. Entry fee is nominal. Go on weekdays to avoid crowds.

name: Shillong Peak
description: At 1965m, Shillong Peak is the highest point in the state. A military-controlled zone offering a panoramic 360° view of the city, Bangladesh plains, and distant hills.
tip: Carry your ID — it's a restricted military area. The best views are at sunrise. Permits are issued at the gate.

name: Laitlum Canyon
tagline: End of Hills, Edge of Heaven
description: One of Meghalaya's best-kept secrets, Laitlum Canyon ('End of Hills' in Khasi) offers spectacular views of deep gorges, clouds rolling through valleys, and terraced Khasi villages.
tip: Best experienced in the afternoon when clouds fill the valley. Bring warm layers — it gets cold quickly up top.

name: Dawki & Umngot River
tagline: Crystal Waters at the Edge of India
description: The Umngot River at Dawki is so crystal-clear that boats appear to float in mid-air. This border town with Bangladesh is one of Meghalaya's most surreal destinations.
tip: Visit on weekdays — weekends see long queues for boats. The water is clearest in winter months. Don't miss the suspension bridge.

name: Living Root Bridges
description: The Khasi and Jaintia tribes of Meghalaya guide the roots of rubber fig trees to form living bridges over rivers. The Double Decker Root Bridge near Nongriat is a UNESCO-listed wonder.
tip: Prepare for a steep 3500-step trek down and back up. Start early morning. Hire a local guide from Tyrna village.

name: Cherrapunji (Sohra)
description: Known locally as Sohra, Cherrapunji holds the record for highest rainfall. Home to Nohkalikai Falls (India's tallest plunge waterfall), Mawsmai Caves, and dramatic Khasi landscape.
tip: Waterfall chasing is best in monsoon but driving can be risky. Carry a raincoat even in winter — showers happen year-round.
